1: A revelation of God: the Pharisees' awareness of Christ's growing influence sets in motion the pattern of mounting opposition that will culminate at the cross. The surveillance of Jerusalem's power structures over Jesus is not accidental — it is part of the divine drama in which the religious authorities will ultimately act as instruments of what they intend as destruction but God designs as salvation.
2: A revelation of God: the clarification that Jesus himself did not baptize but his disciples did distinguishes Christ's role from John's. The Baptist baptized with water; Christ will baptize with the Holy Spirit (John 1:33). His abstention from water baptism is consistent with his identity as the one to whom John's baptism pointed — he is the fulfillment, not the continuation, of the preparatory rite.
3: A revelation of God: the withdrawal from Judea to Galilee reflects Christ's pattern of operating according to divine timing rather than external pressure. The phrase "before the hour" (implicit throughout the early chapters) governs his movements. The departure is not flight — it is the measured pace of a mission operating according to a schedule set before the world began.
4: A revelation of God: "he had to go through Samaria" — the verb edei (it was necessary) signals divine compulsion in the Evangelist's idiom. The Samaritan mission is not a detour but a designed component of the one who came to be "Savior of the world" (John 4:42). The God who sent Israel as a light to the nations (Isaiah 49:6) is present in Christ, who now goes to those outside the covenant people.
5: A type: the well Jacob gave at Sychar points forward to what Christ offers — the true inheritance the patriarchs anticipated but could not themselves give. Jacob gave water that satisfies for a moment; Christ gives living water that wells up to eternal life (John 4:14). The patriarchal site becomes the location where the one greater than the patriarchs reveals himself — "Are you greater than our father Jacob?" (John 4:12). The answer is yes.
6: A revelation of God: the Incarnation's full weight is felt here — the one who is the source of all living water sits down, genuinely tired. The Word-made-flesh enters into human physical limitation without reservation. The weariness of Christ at Jacob's well is the weariness of the one who carries the mission of redemption in a fully human body — the same body that will be exhausted on the cross.
7: A revelation of God: Christ initiates the encounter — the giver of living water asks the woman for water. The servant posture of the one who came "not to be served but to serve" (Mark 10:45) is visible here. He crosses the boundary (Jew to Samaritan, man to woman in public) not as an act of social reform but as an enactment of the mission to seek and save what is lost (Luke 19:10).
8: A theme: the disciples' absence buying food creates the narrative space for the encounter, and also sets up the contrast with Christ's "different food" (John 4:31–34). The disciples' preoccupation with physical provision frames their incomprehension of what Christ's true sustenance is — a pattern the Evangelist uses repeatedly to disclose the distance between earthly and heavenly registers.
9: The theme of Christ crossing ethnic-religious barriers anticipates the Gentile mission that the book of Acts will narrate. The "world" of 3:16 includes Samaritans — those who are neither fully Jewish nor fully Gentile. That Christ's first explicit messianic disclosure (John 4:26) is to a Samaritan woman rather than a Jerusalem rabbi dramatizes the scope of the salvation the Evangelist has been describing.
10: A type: "the gift of God" — free, unearned water from a divine source — echoes the gift of manna in the wilderness (Exodus 16:15), which was given freely and had to be received rather than earned. Isaiah's invitation "come to the waters" (Isaiah 55:1) addresses those who are thirsty and have no money — the gift of God comes to those with nothing to offer. Christ is the source of what cannot be purchased.
11: A revelation of God: the woman's assessment — "you have nothing to draw with and the well is deep" — is physically accurate and spiritually inverted. She calculates by the visible means available; Christ offers what requires no equipment, no physical access, no human effort. The encounter dramatizes the discontinuity between the resources of the present order and what Christ freely gives.
12: A direct irony the Evangelist constructs throughout: the woman's challenge — "Are you greater than our father Jacob?" — expects a negative answer but receives a narrative yes. Christ is greater than Jacob, as he will state explicitly in chapter 8 ("before Abraham was, I am"). He gives what Jacob could not: not a well that quenches thirst temporarily, but a spring that never fails.
13: A shadow: all the provisional gifts of the old covenant — manna repeated daily, water struck from rock and consumed, wells dug and drawn from again — were temporary satisfactions pointing toward a permanent gift that the old covenant could not itself supply. Christ identifies himself implicitly as what those provisions were shadows of. Everyone who drinks the old provision "will thirst again" — but not those who drink what Christ gives.
14: A fulfillment: the living water that Christ gives — becoming in the recipient "a spring welling up to eternal life" — fulfills the prophetic images of eschatological water: Isaiah 44:3 (water poured on the thirsty), Isaiah 58:11 (a well-watered garden, a spring that does not fail), Zechariah 14:8 (living waters flowing from Jerusalem). Christ is the source from whom those waters were always going to flow.
15: A revelation of God: even the woman's pragmatic request — "give me this water so I won't thirst or have to keep coming here" — is met by Christ who will give more than she understands. The request is self-interested and misses the spiritual register; Christ does not dismiss it but moves through it toward what she actually needs. The pattern is characteristic: Christ meets people where they are and brings them further in.
16: A revelation of God: "Go, call your husband" is the pivot point of the encounter — Christ moves from abstract teaching to specific personal knowledge. The God who knows his people (Psalms 139:1–4; Jeremiah 1:5) is present in Christ, who knows what is hidden. The shift is not a trap but an act of grace — Christ opens what must be opened before the living water can be received.
17: A revelation of God: the woman's honest answer ("I have no husband") and Christ's affirmation ("you are right") is an encounter with the truth-speaking God who simultaneously exposes and does not condemn. The Johannine pattern continues: Christ does not come to condemn (John 3:17), but his presence surfaces what has been concealed. Encounter with him is always a form of disclosure.
18: A revelation of God: Christ's full disclosure of the woman's situation ("five husbands; the man you now have is not your husband") manifests the omniscience the Evangelist has attributed to him throughout: "he knew what was in man" (John 2:25). This is not exposure for punishment but for encounter — the woman who is fully known by Christ is also fully offered the living water.
19: The theme of Christ as prophet: the woman's recognition of Jesus as a prophet (Deuteronomy 18:15 — the prophet like Moses who speaks God's word) is a step toward the full recognition she reaches in John 4:29 ("could this be the Messiah?"). The Evangelist uses the gradual dawning of recognition as a device — prophet → Messiah → "Savior of the world" (John 4:42) — to disclose who Christ is in ascending registers.
20: A shadow: the entire dispute about geographic worship (Gerizim vs. Jerusalem) is rendered obsolete by Christ's presence. All place-bound worship — temple, mountain, sanctuary — was a shadow pointing toward the reality that Christ announced: the dwelling of God with humanity in person. John 2:21 has already decoded the temple: "the temple he spoke of was his body." Geographic worship gives way to Christ himself.
21: A direct fulfillment announced: "the hour is coming" signals the eschatological moment Christ is inaugurating. The age of place-bound worship — Sinai, Tabernacle, Temple, Gerizim — is closing. The fulfillment is not only future; John 4:23 will add "and has now come." Christ himself is the turning point of the ages, the moment in which the shadow gives way to the substance.
22: The theme of Israel as the covenant channel: "salvation is from the Jews" acknowledges the particular covenant history through which the promise flowed — Abraham, Moses, David, the prophets. Christ himself is the Jew through whom that salvation is given to all peoples (Romans 1:16; John 11:11–12). The particular does not exclude the universal — it is the vehicle through which the universal reaches everyone.
23: A fulfillment: "true worshipers will worship the Father in Spirit and truth" enacts the new covenant of Jeremiah 31:33 (the law written on hearts, not in an external location) and anticipates Joel 2:28 (the Spirit poured out on all). The worship Christ announces is enabled by the Spirit he will send after his glorification (John 7:39; John 20:22). The hour of new-covenant worship arrives with Christ.
24: A revelation of God: "God is Spirit" is the theological disclosure that grounds the new order of worship — God is not spatially confined, not accessible through a single geographic medium, not contained in stone buildings or on particular mountains. This revelation is possible because the Son, who is "one with the Father" (John 10:30) and who has made the Father known (John 1:18), has come to reveal what God is.
25: A direct expectation met: the woman's articulation of messianic hope — "I know that Messiah is coming; when he comes, he will explain everything to us" — is the simplest form of the faith the Gospel calls for. She has the hope; she is about to receive its fulfillment in the most direct possible form: "I am he." Christ does not correct the expectation; he answers it.
26: A direct revelation: "I am he" (egō eimi) is simultaneously the Messianic acknowledgment the woman asked for and the echo of the divine name — Exodus 3:14 (I AM WHO I AM), Isaiah 43:10 ("before me no god was formed"), Isaiah 52:6 ("my people will know that I am he who speaks"). The one standing at Jacob's well bears both the Messianic office and the divine name. This is the deepest self-disclosure in the chapter.
27: A revelation of God: the disciples' surprise at Christ speaking with a woman reflects the social norms of the day — but Christ's disregard of those norms is not incidental. The one who came to seek the lost operates by the standard of the Father's welcome rather than human social hierarchy. His willingness to engage publicly with a Samaritan woman is the Incarnation's social posture made visible.
28: A revelation of God: the abandoned water jar is a small emblem of what an encounter with Christ accomplishes. She came for one thing and received something that made what she came for secondary. The same pattern appears in the calling narratives — fishermen leave their nets (Mark 1:18), Matthew leaves his tax table (Matthew 9:9). Encounter with Christ displaces prior preoccupations.
29: A theme: the woman becomes the first evangelist of the Gospel — she issues the same invitation Philip gave Nathanael ("come and see," John 1:46) and shapes her testimony as a question that invites others to form their own verdict. The mission pattern the Gospel establishes — one person encounters Christ and sends others to him — is inaugurated here at a Samaritan well, before it becomes the church's pattern.
30: The theme of gathering: the Samaritans' movement toward Christ — "they came out of the town and made their way toward him" — is the visible harvest Jesus is about to describe (John 4:35). The OT anticipation of the nations streaming to the Lord's anointed (Isaiah 60:3; John 66:18) is enacted on a small scale here: Samaritans, outside Israel's covenant, are drawn to Christ before he addresses Jewish leaders in Jerusalem.
31: A revelation of God: the disciples' practical care for Christ ("Rabbi, eat something") is appropriate, but their horizon is physical. The contrast they will not grasp (John 4:32–34) is that Christ's sustenance is the Father's will — his food is the work of redemption. The Servant of Isaiah who "delighted to do your will" (Psalms 40:8; Isaiah 42:1) finds his nourishment in the mission itself.
32: A revelation of God: "I have food to eat that you know nothing about" — Christ speaks from a register the disciples cannot yet access. The Evangelist uses their horizontal-level thinking to disclose that Christ operates from a vertical axis: his life is sustained by what flows from the Father, not by what the earth provides. This is the incarnate Son maintaining his relationship to the Father even in human flesh.
33: A revelation of God: the disciples' puzzlement ("could someone have brought him food?") is another instance of the Evangelist's misunderstanding device — it creates the question that allows Christ's full answer to be heard. Their incomprehension is not failure; it is the narrative space in which deeper truth is disclosed. The pattern recurs throughout the Gospel to ensure that Christ's teaching is not taken at its surface level.
34: A revelation of God: "my food is to do the will of him who sent me and to finish his work" expresses the Servant theology of Isaiah 42:1 and John 49:3 — the one who is sent by God finds his sustaining energy in carrying out the mission. Christ is not sustained by bread alone (the temptation of Matthew 4 is the background) but by the Father's word and will. This statement anticipates the cross: "It is finished" (John 19:30) will be the moment when the food is fully consumed.
35: A fulfillment: "look at the fields — they are ripe for harvest" activates the eschatological harvest imagery of Joel 3:13, Isaiah 27:12, and Amos 9:13. The harvest of the last days is not approaching; it is present, because the King who inaugurates it has come. The disciples are being sent into a harvest that Christ's presence has already begun — they do not create it; they participate in what is already underway.
36: The theme of the eschatological gathering: the harvest "for eternal life" places the missionary work within the frame of the age to come. What the disciples gather is not temporary produce but souls for the life of the resurrection age. The sower and the reaper rejoicing together anticipates the final state described in Revelation 21–22 — the harvest complete, all the redeemed gathered.
37: The theme of the long sowing: "one sows and another reaps" names the structure of redemptive history itself. The OT prophets sowed in tears (Psalms 126:5–6); the Baptist prepared the soil; Christ's disciples are now sent to reap. No generation does the whole work. The proverb, quoted as common wisdom, is shown to be a description of how God's plan works across the ages.
38: A revelation of God: "others have done the hard work; you have reaped the benefits of their labor" — the "others" include the whole train of OT witnesses (Moses, the prophets, the Servant-people of Isaiah) who labored without seeing the harvest. Christ sends his disciples into what those others prepared. The entire redemptive history converges at this point — the disciples reap what generations sowed in faith.
39: The theme of faith through testimony: the Samaritans' belief "because of the woman's word" is the first instance in the Gospel of faith coming through witness rather than direct encounter or sign-miracle. The church's mission is anticipated here — as Romans 10:14–17 will frame it, faith comes by hearing. The woman's testimony is the first link in the chain the NT epistles will describe as the normal mode of gospel propagation.
40: A revelation of God: Christ stays among the Samaritans for two days at their invitation. The one who "came to his own, and his own did not receive him" (John 1:11) is received by those outside the covenant people — and he abides with them. The verb menō (to remain/abide) is a Johannine keyword for the deep dwelling-relationship between Christ and those who receive him. Abiding begins here, with Samaritans.
41: A revelation of God: the progression from the woman's testimony (John 4:39) to Christ's own word (John 4:41) maps the two stages of encounter that the Evangelist values — witness brings people to Christ; Christ himself brings them to full faith. The church mediates; Christ is the source. No human testimony, however accurate, is the final resting point — it is an invitation to the encounter with the Word itself.
42: A direct revelation: "Savior of the world" (sōtēr tou kosmou) is the theological climax of chapters 3–4. The Evangelist has built to this from John 3:16 ("God so loved the world") and John 3:17 ("to save the world through him"). The Samaritans — those outside Israel's covenant — are the ones who first speak the title most clearly. Isaiah's vision of salvation reaching to the ends of the earth (Isaiah 45:22; John 49:6) is fulfilled in the one they have encountered at the well.
43: A revelation of God: the departure from Samaria to Galilee continues Christ's pattern of purposeful movement on a timeline that is not determined by human invitation or opposition. His two days with the Samaritans were exactly sufficient; the mission moves forward according to the Father's will. No success detains him beyond the appointed time; no opposition accelerates his departure.
44: The theme of the rejected prophet: "a prophet has no honor in his own country" is the rejection template the Evangelist uses to prepare for what Christ will face from Judea and Jerusalem. The trajectory runs from Elijah and Elisha's rejection in Israel (1 Kgs 17–19; Luke 4:25–27) through Isaiah's commission to a people who will not hear (Isaiah 6:9–10) to Christ himself as the despised and rejected Servant (Isaiah 53:3).
45: A revelation of God: the Galileans' sign-based welcome is a conditional reception — they welcome him because of what they saw, not because of who he is. The Evangelist has already positioned sign-faith as inadequate (John 2:23–25). Their welcome will lead to a deeper encounter, but Christ's assessment (John 4:48) reveals that he is not satisfied with a following grounded only in spectacle.
46: A shadow: the second Cana sign completes the bracket that opened with the first (John 2:1–11). The two Cana signs frame a unit that includes the temple cleansing, the Nicodemus discourse, and the Samaritan encounter — all of which disclose who Christ is. The signs are not isolated wonders; they are numbered markers pointing toward the sign of signs: the resurrection that the Evangelist is ultimately narrating toward.
47: A revelation of God: the royal official's posture before Christ — a man of status reduced to repeated begging — is the posture of faith confronting the boundary of death. Power, wealth, and social position are irrelevant before the limit of mortality. The official's desperation is honest and is met without rebuke; Christ's challenge (John 4:48) addresses the crowd's sign-dependence, not the father's faith.
48: A revelation of God: "unless you see signs and wonders, you will not believe" is Christ's diagnosis of a faith that requires continuous sign-verification. The Evangelist is building toward a faith that rests on the word alone (John 4:50) — what the official will model in the next verse. The challenge is not a refusal but a call to a more durable faith than spectacle-based trust.
49: A revelation of God: the official's persistence despite the challenge — "Sir, come down before my child dies" — is the faith of desperation that bypasses pride and social calculation. He does not argue with Christ's assessment of his generation's faith; he simply keeps asking. The Evangelist presents this desperate, direct appeal as the form of faith Christ will honor: not sign-seeking, but urgent, word-directed trust.
50: A revelation of God: "Go; your son will live. The man believed the word Jesus spoke to him and departed" — this verse is the narrative emblem of the faith the entire Gospel calls for. No sign is performed in his presence; no physical presence, no touch, no dramatic gesture. Christ speaks a word and the official takes him at his word and goes. This is the faith the Gospel presents as the proper response to Christ: belief in his word without requiring visual confirmation.
51: A revelation of God: the confirmation that the healing occurred — servants meet the father with news of recovery — is the retrospective sign that validates the word-based faith of John 4:50. The pattern is: first faith, then confirmation. The Evangelist consistently structures encounters so that believing precedes seeing, not the reverse. Faith is not produced by signs; signs confirm what faith has already received.
52: A revelation of God: the father's verification of the precise hour — "yesterday at one in the afternoon" — is not disbelief but the responsible confirmation of what his faith already received. The precision of the healing (the exact hour Christ spoke) shows that the word of Christ operates with the exactness of divine action — not approximately, not nearly, but at the moment of speaking.
53: The theme of household salvation: "he and his whole household believed" anticipates the pattern that will run through the Acts of the Apostles — Cornelius's household (Acts 10:44–48), Lydia's household (Acts 16:15), the Philippian jailer's household (Acts 16:31–34). One person's genuine encounter with Christ draws others into faith. The Gospel spreads through relational networks, not only through individual conversions.
54: A shadow: the "second sign" notation is the Evangelist's way of counting toward something. Signs 1 and 2 bracket the material between them (chapters 2–4) as the disclosure of Christ's glory. The numbering points forward — the enumeration will not continue indefinitely; it culminates in the sign of signs, the resurrection, which the Gospel has been narrating toward from John 1:1. Each numbered sign is a partial disclosure; the final sign is the full one.
